Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually wait until I felt like I had to do something, then I'd binge through materials for a few hours. I'm a visual learner, so I'd watch the videos and look at diagrams, but I didn't really have a set schedule. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks I'd barely touch anything. It wasn't organized—it was more like controlled chaos.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. If something was due, I'd suddenly become very productive for a day or two. But when there was nothing pressing, I'd drift and do other things—hang out with friends, focus on my extracurriculars. Regular study times weren't really my thing; I just couldn't motivate myself to stick to a schedule when nothing felt urgent.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about an assignment would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, scroll around for a bit, and then leave without doing much. It wasn't like I was excited to be there—it was more like checking a box or making sure I wasn't missing something critical.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, especially mid-semester when things piled up, but it never felt natural or routine. It was always something I did because I had to, not because it was part of my daily rhythm.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and actually see concepts being explained. Some of the diagrams and infographics in the course materials were helpful too. Discussion posts were okay sometimes, but I didn't engage with them much—I just skimmed to see if there was anything useful.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums and most of the optional readings. They felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grades, so I just didn't bother. Some of the supplementary PDFs sat in my downloads folder untouched. If it wasn't going to be on an exam or part of an assignment, I usually skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me get a good grade, I'd do it. But if it was vague or seemed like busywork, I'd lose interest fast. I need to see the point of something, otherwise I just feel bored and unmotivated.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had a deadline and consequences, so I'd do them even if I wasn't feeling it. Optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. There's no pressure with optional activities, and without pressure, I just don't engage.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from my laptop or tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube for explanations on topics I didn't fully understand—sometimes random channels explain things better than the official materials. My notes were all handwritten too, so none of that showed up anywhere.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or going through downloaded slides for hours, but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd also discuss concepts with friends in person or over text, working through problems together. All that learning happened completely off the radar.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then never go back to the platform for that material. I preferred having everything locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the LMS interface. It was just more convenient.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and just felt burnt out and bored with the course content. The stress was building up, and even though I wasn't anxious exactly, I just couldn't bring myself to care. Everything felt repetitive and uninteresting, so I disengaged for a while.
Q13: Definitely. When I had competing responsibilities or was just mentally exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in, stare at the screen, and then close it without doing anything meaningful. Fatigue made it hard to focus, and when I was confused about something, I'd rather just avoid it than struggle through.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video or reading, have it playing in the background while I scrolled through my phone. Technically I was 'engaging' with the material, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. It was just going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. I think it's because I just wasn't motivated—the material didn't excite me, and I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure anyway. Sometimes I'd avoid difficult topics because I didn't want to deal with the frustration of not understanding them right away.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS seriously. Without a deadline, I'd drift. But when something was due in 48 hours, suddenly I'd be logged in, watching videos, downloading materials, and actually working. It's not healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but not a huge one. If I got good feedback, I'd feel okay and keep doing what I was doing. If it was critical, I'd maybe adjust for the next assignment, but I wouldn't go back and review old materials or anything. It didn't really change my engagement patterns much.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard—modules, deadlines, videos, assignments. It didn't really inspire me to engage more than necessary. If the structure had been more interactive or had more visual elements, maybe I would've been more into it. But it felt like every other course, so I treated it like every other course.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. But the heavy reliance on discussion posts and readings made it harder—those formats just don't work for me. Also, having so many online courses at once made it hard to give this one proper attention. Everything blurred together after a while.
Q20: I think sometimes people are just lost or overwhelmed. They log in hoping to figure things out, click around, watch stuff, but can't translate that into completed work. Or maybe they're like me—they engage in bursts but don't follow through because motivation is low. Activity doesn't always equal productivity.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I still got a distinction because I'm good at exams and can cram effectively. I studied a lot offline, and I'm decent at understanding material quickly when I need to. The LMS doesn't capture how efficient someone can be outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you spent on videos—surface-level stuff. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you actually understood anything, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't measure boredom, motivation, or whether someone is just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. For me, it's rare—I mostly just do what's required to get the grade. Real engagement would be curiosity-driven, where you explore beyond the syllabus. I don't think I experienced that much this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored I was most of the time, or how much I relied on offline studying and external resources. It wouldn't capture the conversations with friends that helped me understand concepts, or the fact that my good grades came from efficient cramming rather than consistent engagement. The data tells a very incomplete story.
